Q1: I usually tried to set up a pretty consistent routine, studying about three hours a day spread across the week. Being an auditory learner, I'd often listen to lecture recordings while doing other things or talk through concepts out loud to myself. I had a good setup at home with all the resources I needed, so I'd usually study in my room where I could focus and speak freely without bothering anyone.
Q2: I tried to keep things regular, but honestly, my pattern shifted quite a bit depending on what was due. Early in the semester I was more disciplined, but as things piled up, I found myself cramming more around deadlines. The stress of upcoming exams or assignments would throw off my normal schedule, and I'd end up pulling longer sessions right before something was due.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I needed to check if there were new announcements or when an assignment deadline was coming up. Sometimes I'd go in to watch a lecture video I'd missed or to see if any feedback had been posted. To be honest, I didn't log in just to browse around—it was always purpose-driven, like grabbing what I needed and getting out.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became something I checked automatically like social media or email. I'd remember to log in when I got a notification or when I knew something was due. It felt more like a tool I used when necessary rather than a habit that was part of my daily routine.
Q5: The lecture recordings were absolutely essential for me because I learn best by listening. I'd play them multiple times, sometimes at different speeds, to really absorb the material. The video content felt most aligned with how my brain processes information, so I spent a lot of time with those—over four hours total, which was way more useful than reading through text-heavy documents.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums most of the time. I only posted three times the whole course because it felt performative rather than actually helpful for my learning. I also didn't engage much with supplementary readings or optional materials because I was already stressed and had to prioritize what would directly impact my grades.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what I'd be tested on or if it helped me understand a difficult concept. I valued things that matched my learning style—so audio explanations or video walkthroughs were gold. If something felt like busywork or didn't clearly contribute to my understanding, I'd skip it without much guilt.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. With the stress I was dealing with, I had to triage everything, and optional stuff fell to the bottom of the list. Required activities had this weight to them—I knew I had to do them even when I was scared about my performance. Optional ones just felt like extra pressure I didn't need.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. I downloaded lecture recordings and listened to them on my phone while commuting or exercising. I also had textbooks and would read through relevant chapters while playing the audio in the background. A lot of my studying happened with materials that weren't connected to the LMS at all—notes I'd written, external YouTube videos explaining concepts, that kind of thing.
Q10: There were many times I'd be studying intensively but the LMS wouldn't show it. Like when I'd download a video once and then replay it offline five or six times. Or when I'd spend hours working through practice problems from a textbook. I also did a lot of verbal rehearsal, just talking through concepts out loud, which obviously leaves no digital trace.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture video or PDF once and then work with it offline for days. My internet is reliable, but I preferred having materials saved locally so I could study anywhere without needing to log back in. The LMS would show one download, but that single file might represent hours of actual engagement on my end.
Q12: There were definitely moments when everything felt overwhelming and I got scared about whether I could keep up. Around midterms, the workload from all my courses collided, and I felt paralyzed. The CBT sessions I did helped me work through some of that fear, but there were still days when I'd avoid logging in because I was afraid of seeing what I'd fallen behind on.
Q13: The stress definitely affected how I used the LMS. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd log in less frequently and just grab what I absolutely needed. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars sometimes meant I had to choose between engaging deeply with course materials or just doing the minimum to get by. Fatigue made everything feel harder to process.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd open a video or reading and just zone out completely. My eyes would be on the screen but my mind was somewhere else, worrying about other things. I'd have to go back and re-engage with the same material later because nothing had actually sunk in the first time. The LMS would count that as engagement, but it really wasn't.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments even when I knew they were important. The fear of not doing well enough would sometimes paralyze me, and I'd avoid starting because I was scared of failing. It's this weird cycle where the anxiety about performance makes you avoid the very thing you need to do. I'd distract myself with other tasks that felt safer.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated my LMS usage. I'd see a spike in my activity right before something was due, and then it would drop off again. The pressure of a deadline was often the only thing that could override my fear and get me to actually submit something. Without deadlines, I probably would have engaged even less consistently.
Q17: Feedback was really important to me, but it also scared me. When I got positive feedback, it motivated me to engage more with similar activities. But negative feedback or lower grades would sometimes make me avoid that area of the course for a while. After the CBT sessions, I got better at processing feedback without spiraling, which helped my mood improve.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework to follow, which was helpful because I could see what was expected. But sometimes the pacing felt off—too much content released at once, not enough time to absorb it. The structure helped when it was predictable, but when things changed or extra materials were added, it threw me off and increased my stress.
Q19: Having video lectures available was great for my auditory learning style, so that made engagement easier. But the heavy reliance on discussion posts as a participation metric didn't work for me—I don't learn well through typing out forum responses. Also, the assignment instructions were sometimes confusing, which made me anxious about whether I was doing things correctly.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious or scared about their performance, like me. You might log in repeatedly to check things, rewatch videos, or read instructions over and over because you're worried you missed something. But then the fear of not being good enough stops you from actually submitting work. It's like being stuck in preparation mode forever.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. I performed reasonably well—got a Merit—but my LMS engagement was rated low. A lot of my real learning happened offline, through downloaded materials and external resources. Someone looking at my logs might think I wasn't trying, but I was putting in around 21 hours a week of actual study time that just wasn't visible online.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in and what you click on, but they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually absorbing material or just staring at a screen while your mind races with anxiety. They also miss all the offline work—the textbook reading, the verbal rehearsal, the conversations with classmates outside the platform.
Q23: Being engaged means actually connecting with the material mentally and emotionally, not just going through the motions. For me, it's when I'm actively processing information, asking myself questions, and trying to apply what I'm learning. It's about genuine curiosity and effort, even when you're scared. Logging in and clicking around doesn't equal engagement—it's what's happening in your head that matters.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how much fear and stress influenced my experience, or how the CBT sessions helped me cope better. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures on repeat, or the peer study sessions I had with the 30 people I interacted with outside the formal discussion boards. My journey through this course was much richer and harder than any log could represent.
